title: Burnout Looks Like Cynicism, Not Exhaustion

generation.spec: topic=Burnout & Mindfulness · 7 turns · P2 neutral/blunt · arc=straight_build · use_case=brainstorm->outline->compose->audit · distractor=t3(environment) · verify=no · flagged=no

turns.utterance: Need some angles for a burnout piece. The usual 'self-care' framing is overused.

Here are five angles that sidestep the self-care lane: (1) cynicism as the real marker, not exhaustion, (2) how high performers hide burnout behind productivity, (3) the meeting-to-think-time ratio as a leading indicator, (4) why 'recovery' weeks don't work structurally, (5) the cost difference between preventing and recovering from burnout.
Hold on, someone walked in, one sec. [pause] The cynicism angle's the one, lay out the sections.

Outline for 'Burnout Looks Like Cynicism, Not Exhaustion': (1) The diagnostic error: why we watch for tiredness and miss the attitude shift, (2) What cynicism actually looks like on a team, (3) The inflection point: when detachment crosses into damage, (4) Two practical signals managers can track, (5) One structural fix, not a wellness fix.

source.post: Burnout Looks Like Cynicism, Not Exhaustion

turns.utterance: Draft is up. It runs about 900 words. The diagnostic-error section leads with a hospital analogy that I think fits well, and the structural-fix close avoids the usual 'take a vacation' advice. Read it over when you're ready.
The middle two sections sound like a corporate memo. Run a voice pass.
